claims.text: The Polybius urban legend holds that in 1981 a mysterious arcade game appeared briefly in two Portland arcades, induced migraines, seizures, amnesia, hallucinations, and aggression in players, was serviced by 'men in black' who collected biometric data, and then vanished without a trace — supposedly the result of a covert U.S. government mind-control experiment.

urban legend
about polybius here's how it goes

video game called polybius
that had gameplay unlike anything anyone
had ever seen before
the player would stare at random
geometric patterns and strange shapes
causing them to fall into a sort of
hypnotic state the game was known for
its psychedelic images
unsettling soundtrack and subliminal
messages
and if you play polybius it could cause
all kinds of physiological effects
amnesia anxiety night terrors aggression
insomnia
it could also cause physical effects
like migraine headaches
nausea vertigo even heart attacks and
strokes

sinus lotion
which in german roughly translates to
sense deleting
after playing two portland teenagers
disappeared without a trace
some say they were abducted others say
they were recruited
now this game was so addictive that
players would line up around the block
and get into physical
fights in order to play it people
reported that the polybius machines
were watching players and collecting
data about them
that data was periodically retrieved by
men in black suits
who serviced the machines and the story
goes that these men in black

claims.rationale: This is the canonical urban-legend formulation. No physical Polybius cabinet has ever surfaced, no contemporaneous police, hospital, or arcade-industry record describes it, and the host correctly debunks it later. The compound legend is an instructive example of a folk-narrative that absorbs elements from real-world events nearby in time and space.

claims.text: The Polybius story's only public 'evidence' is one screen capture of a title screen attributed to 'Sinneslöschen' (an awkward German rendering of 'sensory deletion') — but no German company by that name has ever existed, the typography weakly mimics 1980s Williams Electronics conventions, and earlier-than-1994 mentions in Usenet archives cannot be substantiated.

again

well actually more than most people

claims.rationale: All the host's debunking points are accurate. The 'Sinneslöschen' name is famously bad German (the proper word would be 'Sinnesentzug' or similar). No German company by that name was ever incorporated. The Williams-font similarity is a real observation in the Polybius-research community. Pre-2000 Usenet mentions are not findable in Google Groups archives despite multiple researchers searching.

claims.text: Several real, documented arcade-related medical incidents from the early 1980s — Michael Lopez's 1982 migraine/seizure on Tempest at the Beaverton Malibu Grand Prix, Jeff Daly's fatal heart attack on Berserk, Peter Bukowski's similar Berserk death in Calumet City, the Calumet City stabbing — provided real source material that crystallized into the Polybius legend.

evidence.quote:
michael lopez was playing tempest with
friends at the malibu grand prix arcade
in beaverton oregon
he began to feel a weird sensation in
the back of his head
and he started losing his vision he
became dizzy stumbled outside the arcade
and threw up all over the parking lot he
then collapsed a few blocks from his
home screaming in pain
now apparently the game's flashing
lights and visual effects triggered a

okay but then there's the case of jeff

berserk
that same year the daily had an unknown

apparently the stress of trying to break
the game's high score triggered a heart
attack

also in calumet city two teenage boys

then there's brian morrow who was
playing asteroids for 28 straight hours
trying to break the record
he got sick and collapsed now mauro was
pounding coca-colas for h

claims.rationale: All four incidents are documented in contemporaneous newspaper coverage. Lopez's 1982 Tempest seizure appeared in The Oregonian; Daly and Bukowski are the most-cited 'killed by Berserk' cases of 1981-82, both extensively reported. The crucial historiographic point — that real news clippings provided the substrate the Polybius myth later organized — is well-supported.

claims.text: The FBI really did install hidden microphones and cameras inside Tempest arcade cabinets in the early 1980s as part of sting operations targeting drug dealing, gambling, and petty crime in Pacific Northwest arcades — to the point that the Bureau temporarily caused a regional shortage of Tempest cabinets.

pacific northwest

claims.rationale: The FBI arcade surveillance operations of the early 1980s are documented in FOIA-released records and in subsequent journalistic investigations (notably by Cat DeSpira / Retro Bitch and by Brian Dunning at Skeptoid). The Tempest-cabinet preference and the regional-shortage anecdote are part of the standard published account. Some specifics may be slightly inflated but the core is real.

claims.text: MK-Ultra — the CIA's covertly funded mind-control research program run by chemist Sidney Gottlieb in the 1950s and 60s — really did use psychological torture, electric shock, and high-dose LSD on subjects in U.S. universities, prisons, and overseas detention centers including in Japan, Germany, and the Philippines, and the human cost is unmeasurable because the CIA destroyed most of the program records in 1973.

the files

claims.rationale: All documented historical fact, established by the 1975 Church Committee and the 1977 Kennedy Senate hearings. Sidney Gottlieb did head MK-Ultra; the program did include LSD experiments, psychological torture, and overseas safehouse experimentation; CIA Director Richard Helms ordered the records destroyed in 1973. The known surviving documentation is a fraction of what once existed.

claims.text: The 'Polybius' game that exists today as freeware is a 2007 fan-made tribute by Rogue Synapse — explicitly designed to recreate the legend's psychedelic visuals and subliminal messages — not the original game, and its existence is sometimes confused for evidence the legend is real.

known for creating fictional arcade
games like the one from the last
starfighter
they created a game named polybius in
2007 and their version attempts to
recreate the gameplay described in the
urban legend
including psychedelic visuals and
subliminal messages it's pretty creepy
it's kind of fun i'll link it below you
can play it's free yeah

from the 80s where an intergalactic
space force uses a video game to recruit
star pilots
this probably leaked into the polybius
legend allegedly players were
disappearing
and it wa

claims.rationale: Rogue Synapse (run by Sean Patrick Cooper) really did publish the freeware Polybius reconstruction in 2007; it's still available. The Last Starfighter (1984) really did seed the 'aliens recruit gamers' meme. Both correctly attributed.

claims.text: The choice of the name 'Polybius' is itself a sly tell — the real Polybius was a 2nd century BC Greek historian known for the Polybius square cipher and famous for arguing that nothing should be believed without verifiable evidence and witnesses, suggesting the legend's author was deliberately trolling conspiracy theorists.

greek philosopher
born in 200 bc in megalopolis

cryptography and you might have heard of
the polybius square which is a type of
cipher named for him
polybius also famously said you should
never believe anything
unless it could be verified with hard
evidence and witnesses
so yeah i think whoever started this
story was trolling us
now the urban legend of polybius emerged
in february of 2000
when a listing for the game showed up on
coinop.org a

claims.rationale: Polybius was a real Greek historian (c. 200-118 BC, born in Megalopolis), the Polybius square is a real classical cipher, and his Histories really do emphasize evidentiary rigor and eyewitness corroboration. Whether the choice of name was deliberately ironic vs. coincidental is unprovable but the host's reading is plausible and clever.

claims.text: The Polybius urban legend in its modern form is precisely traceable to a February 2000 listing on the arcade-game database coinop.org by site owner Kurt Koller, which was then amplified by GamePro magazine coverage and a Slashdot post — all the apparent 'historical' claims about the game post-date this 2000 listing.

claims.rationale: The coinop.org / Kurt Koller / February 2000 origin point is the canonical investigative finding — first documented by Cat DeSpira's 'I Polybius' research and later corroborated by Skeptoid and others. The amplification chain through GamePro and Slashdot is documented. This is the strongest single piece of evidence that the Polybius story is a 21st-century invention rather than a remembered 1981 event.

claims.text: Steven Roach, who claimed to have worked on Polybius and posted detailed insider accounts on coinop.org, is a documented hoaxer whose story has been investigated and disproven — illustrating how an unsolved internet mystery reliably attracts fabricated insider claimants.

claims.rationale: Steven Roach's 2003-2007 'I worked on Polybius' posts on coinop.org are real and have been thoroughly debunked — he could not produce any verifiable details consistent with his claimed role, and his account contradicted itself across iterations. This is one of the clearest examples of the Polybius story attracting fabulist claimants.

claims.text: The host concludes that no Polybius arcade game ever existed, but that all the constituent threads — addictive games causing health crises, FBI surveillance via arcade cabinets, CIA mind-control programs — are individually true and were occurring in approximately the same place and time, which is why the urban legend works as well as it does.

claims.rationale: The host's final synthesis is essentially correct and constitutes a sophisticated take on the urban legend: the constituent claims are true individually, the legend assembles them into a story that doesn't actually correspond to a real combined event. This kind of 'urban legend as folkloric processing of true ambient context' analysis is consonant with how folklorists like Jan Brunvand have theorized similar legends.
